data.title: Okeanis Eco Tankers: Stronger Fundamentals, But Now A Geopolitical Trade
data.description:
When I first wrote an article about Okeanis Eco Tankers (ECO), the thesis was quite simple, especially if we compare it to today. It was and still is a modern tanker operator, which I consider to be one of the most effective in the sector. The company at the time had new VLCC and Suezmax fleet, which allowed them to generate around 35% EBIT margin and around 22% net profit margin. Many of it's competitors never reached these kind of numbers. Also, ROE was around 19% which immediately put it on my screener and was almost double the sectors average. At the time of my article it was a good cyclical buy, as it was high quality stock which was not priced in yet.

Since then, the situation has changed quite a lot. The stock went from $25 to around $50 level. It is now quite obvious that part of the upside is already realised. The market started valuing this business and the sentiment become more bullish on shipping stocks. This is apparent especially now when the tanker rates are high and the companies results are better than expected. As of now, the company is not a play for me anymore, yet I believe it has some upside potential. I like stocks that are overlooked, which this company is not anymore, ECO is now more on the radar.

The most important change however is not in the price, but in overall setup. When I wrote an article it was cheap cyclical bet with clear upside logic. Now, I believe it is more of a geopolitics play. Why? Because earnings are now dependent more on events in Middle East region than from cycle. In other words, ECO is now more of a geopolitics driven play than a tanker company play.

Business

If we look at the results themselves, the company is in better condition since my first article. Q4 2025 was a strong quarter, which mainly was driven by higher tanker rates. TCE earnings increased to $76 thousand a day, which is completely different regime than a year ago. It is important to understand, that this a spot market effect and we cannot trust it that the results will continue to be that good. Given that major part of the fleet is in the spot market, they can catch the rates fast and it can immediately be seen in profits.

Balance is also a little bit better, but it is not clean. The cash increased to almost $120 million, which gives flexibility. But at the same time ECO remains leveraged with significant debt level. It is common in this sector to be fair. By the end of 2025 the company initiated equity issuance ($130 million) to finance their fleet expansion. And this is the part I do not like - shareholder dilution. In short, we see better balance sheet, but part of this greatness comes not from operations, but from new capital.

Their fleet also changing and quite well. The company is moving from 14 ships to bigger fleet, and has now 16 tankers, and plans additional Suezmax newbuilds. From what I gathered we should see new vessels in 2026. This in turn means better earning capacity in the future. It is good that the company has modern, emissions regulation compliant ships that I consider a competitive advantage of theirs.

However, I am as always very sceptical. The bigger the fleet, means the bigger spot exposure, which means earning leverage. When the rates will go up, the profits will go up too. Tricky part is that it works in the opposite way the same. With better fleet, the company becomes higher beta play on tanker market.

War Impact

The conflict in Middle East is not really good for tanker sector as we already know. It affects one of the main global oil supply points. Some of the readers will be already informed, some might not, but Strait of Hormuz is considered one of the most important chokepoints in the world. One fifth of the global oil trade goes through this point. As of now, the risk is huge, we see stocks collapsing, then going back up and back down. Any problems in the region I mentioned tend to spill over from oil price to physical transportation.

I do not want to get political, but I will have to state the obvious. The oil price is not really the point here. When Middle East flows become less reliable and more complicated. Buyers, especially from Asia start to look for alternatives. And we have players like U.S., Brasil, West Africa. The problem is that these regions are quite far. Reality is that the longer the trip, the more time it takes to deliver the cargo and fleets become less effective. In practice, I have noticed historically that just means less available fleet in the market and pressure on spot tariffs.

I might sound unethical, but why is it good for this company? The majority of the fleet is in the spot market, which means that they can immediately start monetising changes like that. Tariffs are going up due to ton-mile inflation and regional problems. ECO earnings should reflect that because due to spot market exposure it moves with the market without huge lag. While, it is my opinion and my opinion only, I believe that the war or situation will change in the region any time soon, the company becomes a good play. Geopolitical shocks like that sometimes generate disproportionately high profits for spot oriented operators.

The risks

This situation that we are currently in is a clear tailwind for the tanker market. But it is very important to understand that this is not just "higher rates = good". The conflict poses risks to operators and to be fair, some of them might start avoiding certain routes, which creates route instability. What it means is that the trips will be longer, but also very ineffective. Another risk that comes into picture is insurance. The ships are like cars, they need to be insured and whenever the risk increases, insurance costs can start putting pressure on profitability. Crew safety regulations tend to change too and sometimes companies are forced to change routes or even stay at the port waiting for safer windows to pass through.

The biggest question for me is now earnings stability for ECO. Their model as we talked is oriented to spot market, which at this time is very good. I feel it is a double-edged sword though. The same mechanism that increases earnings can quickly push it down. If rates start normalising, the revenue might fall as quick as it was going up. Without long-term contracts it is just a dependency on geopolitics as of now. War premium will not be here forever.

Finally, I think it is worth noting that dividends are not guaranteed at all. As of now, it looks attractive and the company is growing, so it might look like a good opportunity. Reality is that the current situation will have to ease off or end one day, meaning that the premium company gets on rates will go down too. Payout comes from the cycle. While the rates are up, dividends look very good. But when the cycle turns, it might be the first thing the company will reduce.

Valuation

As I mentioned, the company is not as cheap as it was in my first article. Looking at forward P/E, ECO is already traded at a premium compared to it's peers. It is even almost at the same level as a mature tanker operators. At the same time we have EV/EBITDA which still looks competitive, but understand that this metric stands on higher earnings cycle point.

We should be looking at balance sheet multiples also. P/B is around 3.15, which is way higher than most of the peers. This shows, that the market has already priced in not only current profitability, but expects it to have the same earnings profile in the future. The problem is that in the tankers sector expectations are rarely stable. Current valuation is only justifiable if the rates remain high (which I believe will). If the cycle goes back to normal, these multiples can contract fast.

Summary

The company today is stronger than at the time I analysed it. The results are better, fleet is expanding, and spot exposure gives opportunity for better profitability. But at the same time, the investment logic changed. It is not undervalued cyclical play anymore, now it is more of a geopolitical bet. The earnings I believe are depending on current market regime. I will be maintaining my rating as a buy, the company is now good for tactical trades, not as a long-term investment.

data.title: Best Buy: Despite Sales Headwinds, Dividend And Low P/E Are Tough To Ignore (Upgrade)
data.description:
2026 has been the year of rather indiscriminate selling in the stock market. Investors have latched onto negative macro headlines, ranging from skyrocketing oil prices and the escalating war in Iran, to a weak consumer economy and the possibility for AI disruption. While we can hardly say that the economy and corporate earnings are on strong footing, we also think the selloff in many stocks has gone too far: and created tremendous buying opportunities in several names.

Best Buy (BBY), the largest pure-play electronics retailer in the U.S., has been dealing with weak comp sales all across last year; that said, the company has maintained market share as the industry declined. The stock, however, is in bear market territory after plunging ~25% from peaks above $84 notched last October, and since January alone the stock has given up a further ~10%.

I last wrote a neutral article on Best Buy in November, when the stock was still trading at $80 per share. Since then, the massive erosion in Best Buy's share price, plus the company's recent dividend increase, have substantially increased Best Buy's appeal for me as an income strategy. While I think that we should certainly keep an eye on weakening recent sales trends, I'm not hesitating to use this juncture as a buy-the-dip opportunity. I'm raising my rating on Best Buy to buy.

Even on flat sales, Best Buy can support its raised dividend

Let's first kick off the discussion with the most attractive piece of Best Buy's narrative today: its dividend yield. Alongside its Q4 earnings print, Best Buy announced a 1% increase in its dividend to $0.96 per share. On an annualized basis, this is $3.84, which at Best Buy's current share prices near $64 translates to a dividend yield of 6.0%.

Best Buy has always been an attractive income play; now, with yields pushing 6% (a huge premium over falling Treasury yields), this stock has become a much more viable income strategy. That said, the core question is: can Best Buy really fund this dividend, given the steep decline in its business?

We find that it can. The chart below showcases Best Buy's cash flow in FY26. Last year, the company generated $1.96 billion in free cash flow, while spending $704 million on capex, leading to $1.26 billion in free cash flow. Meanwhile, the company spent $801 million on dividends and $273 million in share buybacks.

This means the company's dividend payout ratio was a still-healthy 64%; inclusive of buybacks, payout ratio across all shareholder returns was still reasonable at 85%.

Let's now zoom forward to FY27, which is the current year for Best Buy ending in January 2027. For this year, the company is expecting comp sales to be flat (-1% to +1% range), with revenue of $41.2-$42.1 billion also representing ~0% growth at the midpoint.

Importantly, however, Best Buy is still targeting earnings growth this year. It's expecting adjusted operating income of 4.3-4.4%, which is 100bps higher than 3.3% in FY25. The company notes here that it's baking in a 30bps expected increase to full-year gross margins (thanks to higher contributions from ads and Best Buy Marketplace, the company's third-party marketplace business in which it earns commission revenue).

All told, flat revenue and a slightly richer margin profile should lead to improved free cash flow in FY27 (even if Best Buy steps up its capex from $704 million in FY26 to $750 million in FY27).

Comps weakness is targeted to certain categories, and Best Buy is still maintaining share

Of course, we can't ignore the core risk that Best Buy faces: the recent downturn in sales trends. In Q4, the company generated $13.81 billion in sales, down -1% y/y and missing Wall Street's exceptions of $13.88 billion (closer to flat y/y) by a one-point margin.

Comp sales, meanwhile (which measures the revenue growth from existing stores, and excludes the contribution of newly opened or recent closed stores) fell at -0.8% y/y domestically, and an even sharper -1.3% overseas.

There is good news here, however. Weakness has largely been confined to a few categories. Appliances, consumer electronics, and entertainment (which together represent just shy of 50% of the company's overall revenue) are the major decliners; that said, Best Buy is still enjoying voracious demand for mobile phones and computers, which achieved mid single digit comp sales growth in the U.S.

There's certainly cyclicality in buying patterns, especially for large items like appliances (and we're still facing headwinds from a weak housing market as well, which is usually the impetus for buying large appliances like dishwashers) . We note, importantly, that Best Buy believes it has at least maintained (if not grown) market share, and the company also notes that sales trends improved across December and January. Its guidance for Q1 calls for a return to positive +1% comps growth. Per CEO Corie Barry's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Our Q4 comparable sales were down 0.8% versus last year, within our guidance range for the quarter. Our data sources show our market share was at least flat, pointing to slightly softer consumer demand for our industry during the holiday quarter. Our holiday customer demand patterns were also different than modeled, despite sales event timing that was very similar to last year's. We saw softer-than-expected sales in November and the beginning of December. We then experienced strong sales in the last 2 weeks of December and the start of January, and sales were negatively impacted by weather-induced store closures during the last week of the quarter.

We were prepared for a promotional holiday and the environment was even a bit more promotional than we factored heading into the quarter."

Again, we think pessimism is already baked into the stock, and we like that even at a flat sales profile, the company can still readily support its increased dividend.

The GameStop variable; risks and key takeaways

There's one final variable that's worth mentioning: recently, speculation has increased that GameStop (GME) may be looking to acquire Best Buy outright. GameStop's Ryan Cohen has mentioned that he is looking to make a transformational acquisition to turn GameStop around.

This scenario has become, in my view, far more likely with the recent buildup in GameStop's balance sheet and the erosion in Best Buy's market value. See the snapshot below: as of the end of Q4, GameStop carried $9.0 billion in cash (not to mention $368 million in Bitcoin that is marked to market; though Gamestop gets a lot of attention for its crypto holdings, its cash balances are actually far more vast).

At current share prices near $63, Best Buy has a market cap of $13.5 billion, and a lower enterprise value of $12.9 billion (the company has $1.74 billion in cash offset against $1.17 billion in debt, or a ~$0.57 billion net cash position). Given both GameStop and Best Buy's clean balance sheet, the deal looks feasible with only a modest amount of debt thrown in on top of GameStop's balance sheet.

Of course, there are risks we need to keep in mind here. It's possible that Best Buy's stock today is being propped up, at least slightly, by hopes of an acquisition - and if GameStop announces an alternative target, Best Buy shares could falter. We should also keep an eye on weakening comp sales trends and a tepid consumer economy, as Best Buy's guidance for FY27 relies on at least flat comps (which would be an improvement to Q4's declines).

That said, we find risks to be priced into the stock, with Best Buy trading at just a 10.0x FY27 P/E (based on the midpoint of its $6.30-$6.60 pro forma EPS guidance for this year) and yielding a healthy ~6.0% to reward investors for patience.

Use the dip here as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Jumps Amid Trump's Escalation Warning
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist | Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Energy – Oil jumps after Trump’s war escalation threats

Oil rebounded sharply on Thursday, rising more than 5% after two days of declines, as US President Donald Trump threatened a further escalation of the war with Iran, injecting fresh uncertainty into energy markets.

Brent was trading above $107/bbl, while WTI was close to $106/bbl on Thursday morning following Trump’s address to the nation last night. The President said the US would hit Iran “extremely hard” over the next two to three weeks and could target “each and every one” of the country’s power plants. He also claimed the Strait of Hormuz would reopen “naturally” once the conflict ends, though he offered no details or a clear timeline. Trump additionally urged allies reliant on Middle Eastern energy to help resolve the near‑closure of Hormuz.

Even if shipping through the Strait of Hormuz resumes, a return to prewar market conditions is likely to be slow, as upstream production restarts, logistics normalisation and inventory rebuilding will take time.

US inventory data were mixed. EIA figures showed crude stocks rose for a sixth consecutive week, increasing by 5.5m barrels, below the 10.3m‑barrel build reported earlier by the API. Total crude inventories climbed to 461.6m barrels, the highest since June 2023 and close to the five‑year average. Cushing stocks rose by 520k barrels to 31.5m barrels, also the highest since July 2024. Crude imports were steady at 6.5m b/d, while exports increased by 199k b/d to 3.5m b/d.

In refined products, gasoline and distillate stocks fell by 0.6m barrels and 2.1m barrels, respectively, reflecting weak refinery runs despite rising implied demand amid heightened supply concerns. Refinery utilisation slipped 0.8ppt week-on-week to 92.1%.

Metals – Aluminium rallies on more Gulf smelter halts

Aluminium prices rallied above $3,500/t on Thursday after reports that Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) halted operations at its Al Taweelah smelter after the site was hit by Iranian missiles and drones over the weekend, according to Wood Mackenzie. According to the report, utilisation at Alba’s smelter has fallen to around 30%, from 81% previously. For now, these reports have not been confirmed by the companies.

If confirmed, a halt at EGA’s 1.6 mtpa Al Taweelah smelter, Alba’s reduced operations and earlier curtailments at Qatalum would take around 3 mtpa of capacity offline – close to half of Middle East aluminium production. That marks a sharp escalation from the roughly 8-9% disruption we were flagging in mid‑March.

This would imply materially deeper aluminium deficits across all our scenarios, lifting the severe disruption case to around 2-2.5 Mt, with even the base case now pointing to a deficit of roughly 1 Mt.

With Chinese supply capped and Indonesia’s ramping up constrained by power and limited capacity growth elsewhere, aluminium’s fundamentals remain firmly supportive. Prolonged Middle East disruptions add further tightening risk.

EGA has also reportedly moved to sell large volumes of alumina in the wake of the strike, according to a Bloomberg report.

LME aluminium prices are up around 17% year‑to‑date, with the forward curve in steep backwardation. That said, escalating global growth concerns could still lead to some demand destruction, partially offsetting the supply shock.

Aluminium, along with other base metals, pared earlier gains on Thursday morning after President Trump reiterated threats to attack Iran’s power plants, with rising rate risks renewing concerns over demand destruction.

Agriculture – Sugar prices ease on weaker demand signals

Sugar prices fell for a fourth consecutive session, briefly dipping towards US¢15/lb (-3% DoD), as softer oil prices reduced incentives for mills to divert output towards ethanol. While the front‑month contract is still up almost 12% over the past month – its strongest run since 2023, supported by crude’s earlier rally on Middle East tensions – recent hopes of de‑escalation have weighed on energy markets and, in turn, on sugar. Elevated volatility and uncertainty around the timing of any resolution have also encouraged position‑trimming. Meanwhile, favourable weather conditions in Brazil continue to support cane development, reinforcing expectations of ample supply in the upcoming season.

In India, data from the National Federation of Cooperative Sugar Factories show 2025/26 sugar production up 9% year-on-year to 27.1mt as of 31 March. The number of operating mills fell to 74, from 113 a year earlier, while cumulative cane crushing rose to 283.5mt, up from 265.5mt over the same period.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Total Return Forecasts: Major Asset Classes - April 2, 2026
data.description:
The war with Iran started over a month ago and could run for several more weeks, according to President Trump’s address to the nation last night. The short-term effects for markets have already been substantial, and more turbulence is potentially brewing for the near-term outlook. But even a month of war has yet to meaningfully change long-term expected returns for the major asset classes overall.

If financial markets continue to fall, at some point, the decline will lift long-run performance expectations by more than a trivial degree. But in the context of our modeling for the long-term horizon (outlined below), last month’s slide in asset prices has been a marginal factor.

Today’s updated long‑term forecast for the Global Market Index (GMI) is relatively steady at a 7%-plus annualized total return. GMI’s projected long‑run outlook continues to run well substantially below its trailing ten‑year performance. It all adds up to a case for managing expectations down for performance relative to recent history – a shift that predates the war and, for now, doesn’t appear likely to change in the immediate future.

GMI is a market‑value‑weighted mix of the major asset classes (excluding cash) via ETF proxies. The forecast is calculated as the average of three models (defined below). The current 7.2% annualized estimate for GMI is slightly below the previous estimate and remains well below GMI’s trailing 9.0% annualized return for the past decade.

Roughly a third of GMI’s components are projected to generate weaker returns relative to their respective results over the past ten years (indicated by the red boxes in far-right column below). The same subpar performance applies to GMI, which is currently projected to earn a materially softer return compared with its realized performance for the trailing ten‑year window through March.

GMI represents a theoretical benchmark for the “optimal” portfolio that’s suited for the average investor with an infinite time horizon. Accordingly, GMI is useful as a starting point for customizing asset allocation and portfolio design to match a particular investor’s expectations, objectives, risk tolerance, etc. GMI’s history suggests that this passive benchmark’s performance will be competitive with most active asset-allocation strategies, especially after adjusting for risk, trading costs and taxes.

It’s likely that some, most, or possibly all of the forecasts above will be wide of the mark to some degree. GMI’s projections, however, are expected to be somewhat more reliable vs. the estimates for its components. Predictions for the specific markets (US stocks, commodities, etc.) are subject to greater variability compared with aggregating the forecasts into the GMI estimate, a process that may reduce some of the errors through time.

Another way to view the projections above is to use the estimates as a baseline for refining expectations. For instance, the point forecasts above can be adjusted with additional modeling that accounts for other factors and assumptions not used here. Customizing portfolios for a specific investor, to reflect risk tolerance, time horizon, and so on, is also recommended.

For perspective on how GMI’s realized total return has evolved through time, consider the benchmark’s track record on a rolling 10-year annualized basis. The chart below compares GMI’s performance vs. ETFs tracking US stocks and US bonds through last month. GMI’s current return for the past ten years is a robust annualized 9.0%.

Here’s a brief summary of how the forecasts are generated and definitions of the other metrics in the table above:

BB: The Building Block model uses historical returns as a proxy for estimating the future. The sample period used starts in January 1998 (the earliest available date for all the asset classes listed above). The procedure is to calculate the risk premium for each asset class, compute the annualized return and then add an expected risk-free rate to generate a total return forecast. For the expected risk-free rate, we’re using the latest yield on the 10-year Treasury Inflation Protected Security (TIPS). This yield is considered a market estimate of a risk-free, real (inflation-adjusted) return for a “safe” asset — this “risk-free” rate is also used for all the models outlined below. Note that the BB model used here is (loosely) based on a methodology originally outlined by Ibbotson Associates (a division of Morningstar).

EQ: The Equilibrium model reverse engineers expected return by way of risk. Rather than trying to predict return directly, this model relies on the somewhat more reliable framework of using risk metrics to estimate future performance. The process is relatively robust in the sense that forecasting risk is slightly easier than projecting return. The three inputs:

* An estimate of the overall portfolio’s expected market price of risk, defined as the Sharpe ratio, which is the ratio of risk premia to volatility (standard deviation). Note: the “portfolio” here and throughout is defined as GMI

* The expected volatility (standard deviation) of each asset (GMI’s market components)

* The expected correlation for each asset relative to the portfolio (GMI)

This model for estimating equilibrium returns was initially outlined in a 1974 paper by Professor Bill Sharpe. For a summary, see Gary Brinson’s explanation in Chapter 3 of The Portable MBA in Investment. I also review the model in my book Dynamic Asset Allocation. Note that this methodology initially estimates a risk premium and then adds an expected risk-free rate to arrive at total return forecasts. The expected risk-free rate is outlined in BB above.

ADJ: This methodology is identical to the Equilibrium model (EQ) outlined above with one exception: the forecasts are adjusted based on short-term momentum and longer-term mean reversion factors. Momentum is defined as the current price relative to the trailing 12-month moving average. The mean reversion factor is estimated as the current price relative to the trailing 60-month (5-year) moving average. The equilibrium forecasts are adjusted based on current prices relative to the 12-month and 60-month moving averages. If current prices are above (below) the moving averages, the unadjusted risk premia estimates are decreased (increased). The formula for adjustment is simply taking the inverse of the average of the current price to the two moving averages. For example: if an asset class’s current price is 10% above its 12-month moving average and 20% over its 60-month moving average, the unadjusted forecast is reduced by 15% (the average of 10% and 20%). The logic here is that when prices are relatively high vs. recent history, the equilibrium forecasts are reduced. On the flip side, when prices are relatively low vs. recent history, the equilibrium forecasts are increased.

Avg: This column is a simple average of the three forecasts for each row (asset class)

10yr Ret: For perspective on actual returns, this column shows the trailing 10-year annualized total return for the asset classes through the current target month.

Spread: Average-model forecast less trailing 10-year return.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: WOOD: Outperformance Unlikely Due To Structural Weakness In Timber Industry
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My last article on iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF (WOOD) was published nearly two years ago, and my prediction of poor long-term performance for the fund has proven so far correct. Its 12% capital loss since my previous article compares extremely poorly with the 29% enjoyed by the broad market over the same period (as proxied by the S&P 500).

Nevertheless, I thought that, given the composition of the ETF changing significantly since my last coverage, it would be worth re-appraising the fund and looking at whether the timber sector (as proxied by WOOD) now represents an attractive value play.

Ultimately however, my up-to-date analysis concludes that ongoing structural weakness in the timber industry renders WOOD an unattractive long-term bet versus the broad market, and may actually increase portfolio risk rather than effectively offering the diversification some investors may hope it provides.

Lumber Prices

Considering that the companies owned by WOOD are mostly concerned with selling material derived from trees, it is not surprising that the ETF's performance is linked strongly with lumber prices. As shown above, although the lines don't overlap perfectly, the correlation is definitely strong, in the same way as the market price of gold miners or oil majors tends to align with the price of the specific commodity they are extracting and selling.

For me though, what is most striking about the graph above is that it demonstrates that over a nine-year period, in terms of capital gains, there has been almost no difference in return between the price of the raw asset (lumber, up 29%), and the aggregate market value of the companies producing it and turning it into products such as paper and packaging (these are up only 30%, per WOOD).

Taking into account total return (i.e. dividend reinvestment), the picture is slightly more optimistic, but it is difficult to argue that a 53% total return from owning equities over the past nine years or so is a successful result, with the S&P offering a 234% return over the same period. What is clear is that given the commodity nature of the timber business, there is a structural issue with these firms being able to generate significant shareholder value, as illustrated by the very small long-term margin between lumber prices and timber equity prices.

The graph since inception (24th June 2008) paints a similar picture, and underscores the reality that over the last couple of decades, there has been little evidence of WOOD's ability to meaningfully compound capital, especially versus the tough comparison of the tech-heavy S&P 500, but also versus small cap stocks (as proxied by the Russell 2000), and indeed by a very significant margin (with even small caps offering close to 3.5x the return of WOOD).

Even the transport sector (as proxied by the XTN ETF), another tough industry dominated by commodity businesses, has performed better than WOOD over the years, illustrating that timber stocks definitively belong to the category of long-term capital destroyers (relatively speaking, versus other sectors).

Holdings

Clearly, it's also important to consider the specific holdings of the fund when considering its investment merit.

Given the fund has "Timber & Forestry" in its name, one might expect it to be a pure-play timer fund, though even the sector breakdown gives some sense of the degree to which this is not the case, with 23% of equities being classed as in the "Containers & Packaging" sector versus "Paper & Forestry Products". This highlights that the fund owns companies at different layers within the timber value chain (some having a much greater emphasis on packaging, for example, which clearly has different economics to pure-play forestry).

Geographically, the fund's largest exposure is to the United States, approaching a third of its assets. Investors should also be aware of some of the country risks associated with some of the emerging market exposures which include Brazil (at ~15%) and China (at ~6%). Taken together, Thailand, Chile and South Africa also represent another ~7% of the fund, illustrating that the fund is absolutely a global timber play, rather than emphasizing only U.S. based firms.

Considering some of the fund's individual holdings is also useful for getting a sense of what prospective buyers will own.

The largest holding at ~8% of assets is Weyerhaeuser (WY), a U.S. timberland REIT with an emphasis on sustainability. Clearly, this is the sort of company one might expect to find in this sort of fund.

On the other hand, Nine Dragons Paper, at 6% of holdings, highlights the degree to which the fund is less of a pure play on timber, but rather on the wider timber value chain and associated industries (packaging). This is a firm which increasingly recovers recycled paper rather than depending purely on wood pulp.

The fund's third largest holding (at ~6% of assets) is UPM, another firm which is increasingly moving into materials outside of simple paper and timber.

As shown above, self adhesives represent an increasing share of revenue, and while they may increase the degree to which value can be added by the firms (rather than following lumber prices), this move into the materials sector can also be seen as diluting the timber exposure offered by the fund.

Geopolitical Risk

The "elephant in the room" concerning all equity coverage at the moment is of course the Iran conflict and the potential effects of that on different sectors.

Interestingly, the effect on WOOD is rather indirect here, and the risk is not that there is a material effect in terms of timber being transported through the Strait of Hormuz. On the other hand, there is a big potential effect in terms of the costs associated with cutting and transporting timber and lumber, where diesel, for example, is a significant input cost.

Perhaps more significantly, housebuilding is a major source of demand for wood products, and the Iran crisis has the potential to increase inflation, leading to frozen or increased interest rates (mortgage rates), which is highly negative in terms of driving the housebuilding construction which the timber industry has been holding out for over recent years.

Clearly then, prospective investors in WOOD need to pay close attention to developments around the Iran conflict and the potential inflation which may result from it, given the likely impacts on the industry.

Dividends

Income investors may be drawn to WOOD on account of its 2.54% dividend yield, which is quite a bit higher than the 1.14% offered by the S&P.

On the other hand, as shown in the graph above, given that the holdings of WOOD vary over time due to rebalancing and reconstitution, this yield can't be relied upon over the long-term. In the second half of 2025, the yield was closer to ~1.5%, which is clearly much less appealing, relatively speaking.

I also think its important to balance the relatively higher yield offered by timber firms against the very poor capital returns I outlined earlier in the article. With returns six times lower than the S&P 500 over less than 20 years, I think it is difficult to argue that the yield is a material consideration here.

Fees

Another aspect which investors should be aware of is the level of fees applied to the fund. While the 0.4% is slightly lower than the 0.5% average across the entire universe of ETFs, it is still at a level which materially erodes returns over time. Over a 10 year holding period, for example, there is a ~4% loss to fees.

One could argue that for a relatively specialized, niche product with international exposure, this level of fees is not totally unresonable. However, in the context of the 0.03% expense ratio from the S&P 500 fund from the same provider (IVV), the fees do seem a little high, especially in view of the very poor long term returns so far offered by the fund.

Upside Risk

Since I've presented a highly bearish view of the fund, it's important to also be aware of the main scenario in which my thesis could be proved wrong. Clearly, the main upside risk here would be a swift end to the conflict in Iran, which would reduce inflationary pressures and reduce the input costs for timber businesses. A calmer geopolitical environment would also leave the path to interest rate reduction clearer, and lower interest rates would be likely to promote the housebuilding which the timber industry relies on.

Conclusion

Overall, WOOD has provided pretty poor results over time, and I think this trend is unlikely to reverse meaningfully in the coming years, especially due to pressures on the industry on account of the current geopolitical situation. I also think the 0.4% expense ratio is not warranted and that the dividend yield should not be a material consideration for investors. Given that the fund's holdings are diversifying into other types of materials (adhesives, for example), I also think there is an argument to be made about the purity of timber industry exposure within the fund.

data.title: Spring 2026 Snapshot Of The S&P 500's Market Cap
data.description:
The market capitalization of the S&P 500 (SPX) shrank in the first quarter of 2026. Picking up from our Fall 2025 snapshot, when the index's market cap was $59.32 trillion, as of the end of 2026-Q1, it has fallen nearly 1.5% to $58.44 trillion for this Spring 2026 snapshot.

In between then and now, the S&P 500's market cap clocked in with a market cap of $60.80 trillion at the end of 2025-Q4, which is the value we would have reported in our Winter 2025 snapshot had we presented it. In the three months since, the total valuation of all 503 stocks included in the S&P 500 has dropped 3.9%.

Much of the change in the index's value during this time was concentrated within the top ten component stocks that make up the benchmark index. At the end of 2025-Q3, they represented 38.9% of the value of the entire index, which increased to 39.2% at the end of 2025-Q4. After the end of 2026-Q1, however, their share of the index has declined to 36.9%.

The following chart shows the relative shares of the top 10 stocks in the S&P 500 at the end of the first quarter of 2026.

Here are the approximate market capitalizations of each of the S&P 500's top ten component firms at the end trading on 31 March 2026:

  • Nvidia (NVDA) $4,184,635,901,084 (7.16%)
  • Apple (AAPL) $3,641,100,791,027 (6.23%)
  • Microsoft (MSFT) $2,700,142,159,638 (4.62%)
  • Amazon (AMZN) $2,226,740,612,169 (3.81%)
  • Alphabet (A) (GOOGL) $1,829,317,090,837 (3.13%)
  • Alphabet (C) (GOOG) $1,694,894,429,210 (2.90%)
  • Meta Platforms (A) (META) $1,449,426,960,152 (2.48%)
  • Broadcom (AVGO) $1,449,426,960,152 (2.48%)
  • Tesla (TSLA) $1,396,826,788,211 (2.39%)
  • Berkshire Hathaway (B) (BRK.B) $1,005,247,730,428 (1.72%)

All ten of these firms have trillion-dollar market caps, with Berkshire Hathaway joining the trillion-dollar club during the past six months.

References

Standard & Poor's. S&P 500 Factsheet. [PDF Document]. 31 March 2026. Accessed 1 April 2026.

SlickCharts. S&P 500 Component Weights. Accessed 1 April 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Medline: A Lack Of Compelling Risk-Reward Makes Me Cautious
data.description:
In an uncertain external environment, shares of Medline (MDLN) have seen a solid first quarter. This came despite selling shareholders offering more shares early in March. Investors are still buying into the long-term growth story, with Medline delivering on solid growth, yet this comes amidst some recent margin pressure, leaving profits largely flat.

All of this is encouraging, and while further growth is seen in 2026, I think that overall valuations are rather demanding, at least too demanding for me, certainly with the namesake business seeing some real margin pressure here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Solid End To 2025

Towards the end of February, Medline reported solid 2025 results, with growth accelerating as the year ended. After full-year revenues rose by 11.5% to $28.4 billion, it really was the final quarter that showed strong momentum, with fourth-quarter sales up nearly 15% to $7.8 billion. That acceleration was largely attributed to the existence of an additional business day in the fourth quarter, seen boosting sales by nearly 2%.

That was the good news, as the continuation of growth meant that the same progress was not seen on the bottom line. The core Medline segment grew full-year sales by nearly 10% to $13.7 billion, with full-year adjusted EBITDA down 2 points to 24.3% of sales, as margin pressure was more pronounced in the final quarter. This was attributed to tariffs, among others, with the full-year headwind from tariffs reported at $290 million. This is just over a percent of sales at large, suggesting that this is part of the reason for margin pressure, but not all of it.

Supply chain solutions revenues rose by 13% for the year, and while earnings were up 50 basis points on the year before, a 5.5% EBITDA margin profile is entirely different from the core segment, which saw real margin pressure. The company reports full-year adjusted EBITDA of $3.47 billion, up some 3% on the year before. Lack of progress here is an area of attention, as the company reported $10.8 billion in net debt as of the year, for a 3.1 times leverage.

The company reported net earnings of $1.16 billion, which is based on GAAP accounting. This, among others, excludes a $704 million amortization charge, which, if added back with statutory tax rates, would result in a pro forma earnings number around $1.7 billion. This is equal to roughly $1.20 per share based on a share tally of about 1.4 billion shares outstanding.

The company guided for continued and modest achievements in 2026, with organic sales seen up 8-9%, and adjusted EBITDA seen between $3.5-$3.6 billion. This suggests to me more of the same dynamic, that of solid topline growth, yet margin pressure severely limiting the progress of adjusted EBITDA numbers.

Selling Shareholders Become Active

Following the expiration of the lock-up period, some investors in Medline sold quite a few shares early in March. Selling shareholders tied to Blackstone, The Carlyle Group, Hellmann & Friedman, and the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority sold some 86.25 million shares at $41, all that ahead of the overallotment option.

Note that Blackstone and its peers got involved with the business in 2021, when they bought the company. They bought the story and real performance of a strong, well-led team. After all, the current management team is really only the second management team in place since the company was founded.

Make Healthcare Run Better

Medline was founded in 1966 and has enjoyed over 50 years of consecutive annual sales growth, driven by the leadership of the Mills family. The company generates some $28 billion in sales, distributing over 300,000 products with some 45,000 employees covering nearly 30 million square feet of warehouse space.

An aging population, prevalence of more chronic diseases, and higher healthcare spending (including outside hospital settings) are long-term drivers for the business. The company serves all spectrums of care, including physician offices, urgent care, surgery centers, laboratories, acute care, hospice, home health, life sciences, and others.

Of course, the company is basically the sum of two segments, with the namesake segment posting far higher margins compared to the supply chain industry business. This is what has been bothering me and, frankly, held me back from getting involved in December when the company went public.

At the time I concluded that demand for Medline's shares was too healthy, after the business went public at $29 per share, as shares rapidly rose to levels in the forties. The company reported a pro forma share count of around 1.4 billion shares, less than I anticipated at the time of the offering, when I only saw earnings of around a dollar per share.

Earnings numbers are now seen around $1.20 per share. Real accretion is seen in 2026 by my math. While EBITDA will only increase modestly, net interest expenses of $812 million in 2025 are seen coming down to $600 million this year. This is equal to about $0.15 per share on a pre-tax basis, creating a realistic roadmap for earnings to advance to $1.30-$1.40 per share, for a 31-33 times forward earnings outlook.

Current Valuations

Trading at $44 per share, the 1.4 billion shares of the business are valued at $62 billion, for a $73 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 2.5 times sales, about 20 times forward EBITDA, and a low thirty-times forward earnings multiple.

That is demanding multiple for any business, even though 10% organic growth looks decent, and Medline's track record is strong. However, the issue, or concern, that I have outside the valuation and leverage discussions is that of the massive discrepancy between segment margins.

Even as the namesake Medline business is facing some current margin pressure, its margins are still seen 4-5 times higher than supply chain solutions. I wonder and fear further reversal of Medline margins, which look very steep (in relation to the more generic products offered).

Given all this, I am leaning cautious here, as I am happy to revisit this stance upon further developments this year, but frankly, I remain hard-pressed to see a compelling risk-reward at current, or even slightly lower levels.

data.title: 7 Dividend Increases Expected In April 2026
data.description:
We are always on the lookout for dividend increases. Whether it is on our website or social media platforms, we are always sharing dividend increase news. In this article, we are featuring 6 expected dividend increases in April 2026. You won't want to miss it!

Why do we suspect these companies are going to announce a dividend increase in April? The dividend stocks featured in this article all have a history of announcing their annual dividend increases in April. The funny thing about large, blue-chip, dividend-paying stocks is that they are generally pretty consistent with dividend announcements. Management teams prefer announcing and paying dividends around the same time every quarter. Plus, shareholders like us also enjoy the predictability and consistency of dividend payments. Especially once you retire and live on your dividend income.

The same consistency applies for dividend increase announcements. For the most part, companies announce their annual dividend increases in the same month every year. (Of course, there are always exceptions to the rule.) Each of the featured dividend stocks, when reviewing their history, have announced dividend increases in April in recent history. That is why we are so confident that each of the featured dividend stocks will once again increase its dividend in April.

Why Dividend Increases Matter

Dividends are the foundation of our goal to reach financial freedom. We continuously invest in dividend growth stocks to grow our passive income. If you haven't figured it out by now, we eat, sleep, and breathe dividends. With high inflation, buying undervalued dividend stocks that increase your dividend is as crucial as ever.

That is why dividend growth is a major deciding factor in all of our stock purchase decisions. It is the third metric of our Dividend Stock Screener for a reason. For each stock, we review a company's history of increasing dividends (consecutive annual dividend increases) and its five-year average dividend growth rate.

Expected Dividend Increases in April 2026

The changes in the economy and the potential impact of tariffs should make the June dividend increases very interesting. Some of the stocks featured in this section could announce changes that deviate from the company's historical average to preserve cash to navigate uncertain waters

In this section, we will present two dividend yields: the current dividend yield as of 4/2/26 and the forecasted dividend yield. The forecasted dividend yield is the potential future dividend yield for the stock if management announces a dividend increase in line with their five-year average dividend growth rate.

Dividend Stock #1: Procter & Gamble (PG)

  • Current Dividend Yield: 2.93% (as of 4/2/26)
  • Forecasted Dividend Yield: 3.10%
  • Consecutive Dividend Increases: 69+
  • 2025 Dividend Increase: 5.47%
  • 5-Yr Average Dividend Growth Rate: 5.97%

Dividend Stock #2: Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)

  • Current Dividend Yield: 2.13% (as of 4/2/26)
  • Forecasted Dividend Yield: 2.24%
  • Consecutive Dividend Increases: 63+
  • 2025 Dividend Increase: 4.68%
  • 5-Yr Average Dividend Growth Rate: 5.18%

Dividend Stock #3: IBM (IBM)

  • Current Dividend Yield: 2.76% (as of 4/2/26)
  • Forecasted Dividend Yield: 2.80%
  • Consecutive Dividend Increases: 23+
  • 2025 Dividend Increase: 0.60%
  • 5-Yr Average Dividend Growth Rate: 1.53%

Dividend Stock #4: Apple (AAPL)

  • Current Dividend Yield: 0.41% (as of 4/2/26)
  • Forecasted Dividend Yield: 0.43%
  • Consecutive Dividend Increases: 13+
  • 2025 Dividend Increase: 4.04%
  • 5-Yr Average Dividend Growth Rate: 4.87%

Dividend Stock #5: Kinder Morgan (KMI)

  • Current Dividend Yield: 3.56% (as of 4/2/26)
  • Forecasted Dividend Yield: 3.64%
  • Consecutive Dividend Increases: 8+
  • 2025 Dividend Increase: 1.74%
  • 5-Yr Average Dividend Growth Rate: 2.19%

Dividend Stock #6: W.W. Grainger (GWW)

  • Current Dividend Yield: 0.82% (as of 4/2/26)
  • Forecasted Dividend Yield: 0.89%
  • Consecutive Dividend Increases: 54+
  • 2025 Dividend Increase: 10.24%
  • 5-Yr Average Dividend Growth Rate: 8.44%

Dividend Stock #7: Costco Wholesale (COST)

  • Current Dividend Yield: 0.52% (as of 4/2/26)
  • Forecasted Dividend Yield: 0.59%
  • Consecutive Dividend Increases: 21+
  • 2025 Dividend Increase: 12.07%
  • 5-Yr Average Dividend Growth Rate: 13.18%

Summary

April will be a very exciting month of dividend increases. So far, in 2026, we have seen low dividend growth as companies favor actions such as cash preservation, share buybacks, and AI infrastructure investments with capital. Hopefully we can see this trend reverse with some of the large Dividend Kings on this list, as they set to announce the dividend increases.

From this list, I own two stocks: Procter & Gamble, Johnson & Johnson, IBM, and Kinder Morgan. That represents 4 of the 7 featured stocks!

What dividend increases are you expecting in April 2026? How many stocks from this list do you own?

- The Dividend Diplomats

data.title: Robinhood: A New Bull Cycle Is Forming (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I was bearish on Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) heading into 2026. Trust me, it was a contrarian take. The fintech had significant 2025, making the list of top performing companies of Russell 1000 throughout the year. Now the stock is down 38% year to date. What happened?

In my opinion, the retail investor-focused company was highly influenced by poor crypto performance. Altcoin season never materialized while both Bitcoin and Ethereum had two consecutive quarters of double-digit decline. As a result, volatility returned and risk appetite decreased.

I strongly believe that the other important factor was broader macroeconomic uncertainty, which fueled Mag 7 underperformance, rotation to metals and more defensive sectors, and multiple contraction. The S&P500 forward earnings multiple contracted from 23x few months ago to below 20x. Surely enough, that's a big drop in a short period of time.

Now, on the upside, I think that a lot of concerns may already be in the rearview mirror. That's why I am changing my view on Robinhood stock, which I honestly find quite compelling at these levels.

I upgraded the rating from Sell to Buy. Let me present to you the reasons behind my thesis.

Fundamentally, the Business Is Growing Strongly

The recent drop in the stock price could make one think that business experienced a massive shift in performance, but that's not really the case. Sure, sentiment around the stock shifted, but fundamentally Robinhood keeps improving.

A few months ago, the fintech shared its total platform assets were up in 2025 by 68%. That's significant improvement. Of course, we need to highlight that it dropped on a quarter-over-quarter basis by 3%, but I think that's mainly due to broader markets decline across both crypto and stocks. I wouldn't be surprised if this metric stagnates in the following earnings call as well, supported by the recent cross-asset sell-off, mainly driven by the conflict in the Middle East.

The fintech managed to achieve an all-time high in funded customers. That's 27 million. Robinhood experienced a 15.5% increase in Gold-tier adoption rate, a 5% increase versus the same quarter last year. So, the general trend in performance is up.

What I also love about its recent performance is that there's a clear indication of a sticky business model. Now to support this, I'd love to point out that the average total platform assets per funded customer increased to $12,000. Although it may not seem like a lot, it indicates nearly triple increase over the past 24 months. Adoption continues.

On the other hand, sure, bears could say that the trend also shows the first quarter of this metric's decline. Could it signal that it peaked 6 months ago at $12.4k? Personally, I don't think so. As pointed above, I anticipate asset-related performance deterioration in the upcoming report, but that's because of the recent market turbulence. It's a short-term dip in my honest opinion.

Robinhood indicated market share increase across all operating markets. That supports my bullish take. And yes, it also resonates with the sticky business argument. It simply tells me that customers are happy with HOOD's offering. They are favoring the platform versus alternatives.

Retirement accounts held at Robinhood increased by 60% on a year-over-year basis, and I believe this was the most significant achievement of the management team. On a forward-looking term, to me it says that Robinhood is building a strong base of clients that are trusting their retirement funds with the company. Such savings are typically held at specific companies for years, and are not easily moved elsewhere. That's a big tailwind for the fintech in the years ahead, which makes me think it should be able to keep up with analysts estimates of double-digit top line growth over the next few years.

As I said, fundamentally, Robinhood remains strong company. Not only that, but it continues increasing market share, attracts new customers, and introduces retirement offering, which I think supports long-term tailwind. That's surely not a declining business. It's at the forefront of digital financial services.

What to expect in the upcoming earning calls?

In a few weeks HOOD is expected to share Q1 2026 results. Analysts expect top and bottom-line double-digit growth. Personally, I think it's likely going to be a mixed quarter due to broader market pressure and impact on assets under management. I wouldn't expect a double beat, but that's surely a possibility.

I will be forward looking to funded and retirement account figures the most. I want to see the broader trend intact, which would give me confidence in HOOD's future performance. To that, if the fintech maintains operational costs suppressed, this could lead to elevated margins. Surely enough, that would be great.

Crypto Could Spark Rebound

Crypto hasn't been great. And that's a nice way to put it. Both Bitcoin and Ethereum experienced severe drops over the past two quarters.

The tables below don't indicate altcoin performance, but it's been a while since I've seen a bullish take on altcoins. Quite frankly, for a reason, as some of the coins, such as WIF-USD are down by nearly 80% over the past 6 months.

Now Robinhood is known to be a retail-driven platform. Naturally, everyday investors felt the sentiment change in crypto space, which had negative effect on risk appetite, assets under management, and crypto activity in general. But I argue this could change soon.

Historically, we haven't had more than 3 negative quarters for Ethereum. Bitcoin had a streak of four in 2022, but it also correlated with the severe broader bear market. If that's not on the table as the base case, we could argue that this crypto shake-out is closer to its end than the beginning, right?

Also, historically, Q2 has been the most bullish quarter for Ethereum with a 15% median return. For Bitcoin that's the second-best quarter with a median return of roughly 7%. The seasonality argument remains in-play, and this could spark a rally for both crypto and Robinhood.

There are a few additional tailwinds which could materialize in months to come. As we know, the Fed will have a new chair in May, and his base agenda leans towards a rate cuts in 2026. Well, that's not a base case presented by interest rate forecasts for the next 8 months, which makes me argue the market hasn't priced this tailwind in. The market views no cuts for the year. Of course that's possible, but I don't think it's likely. President Trump publicly stands for a cut, and unless inflation skyrockets, the new Fed may be favoring easing after the fastest interest rate hike cycle over the past 40 years.

Quite frankly, the Clarity Act could be a double-edged sword for the cryptocurrency market. Highly depending on the outcome of the bill, and whether it results in significant digital asset and stablecoin adoption, crypto sentiment could swing both ways. But a positive outcome would lay ground for its future success in my opinion.

Lastly, if Middle East conflict comes to an end, that should be a bullish catalyst for the broader markets. The risk appetite may improve, alongside investor sentiment, and this could ignite a rally for both crypto and Robinhood stock.

Down To Earth Valuation While Growing At Double-Digit Pace

Not so long ago, Robinhood was above 60x forward P/E stock. Now the earnings multiple contracted. It reached down to earth levels of roughly 26x to 28x, which makes me think it's fairly cheap at the current price tag. Why's that?

Well, the fintech has delivered 50% revenue growth over the past 12 months, outperforming the sector median of nearly 9% by almost 5 times. Quite frankly, it outperforms the historical average of the companies operating in the S&P500 at 10% by 5x, too. In my honest opinion it deserves a premium multiple. It suggests undervaluation considering HOOD trades at 27x forward P/E versus the benchmark's 20x.

To this point, analysts expect 5.9% revenue growth for S&P500 in 2026. Analysts think Robinhood can deliver 20% top line increase in the same period. That's another argument for a premium multiple, and comparing apples to apples, it signals undervaluation to me.

HOOD reached a 14% return on equity over the past 12 months, a nearly 3.9x outperformance versus peers at approximately 4%. It also operates significantly above industry's gross and net income margins. For instance, it posted a 92% gross margin on a TTM basis, almost 50% advantage versus peers at 62%. Also, a 42% of net income, a 74% difference to sector at 24%. Let's admit, that's not a meaningless few bps outperformance. Simply put, Robinhood is highly efficient business, and that deserves multiple expansion.

Now I don't think HOOD is likely to return to its recent valuation anytime soon. That'd be a stretch. But I wouldn't be surprised if the fintech traded at about 35x earnings multiple in months to come.

Why I Could Be Wrong And Decline May Continue

Despite my shift from bearish to bullish, there are obvious risks that could meaningfully impact stock price performance in both the short and long term.

As indicated, I think crypto sentiment significantly affects HOOD's stock price performance. I anticipate a rebound in crypto prices, but that's obviously not a guarantee. My research is pointing to favorable seasonality, but there's a chance it won't play out. If that were to happen, the stock could decline further.

Macroeconomic concerns are not out of the picture. Although President Trump communicates that we are not that far from the finish line in the Middle East, tensions continue. Concerns tied to high oil prices and rebound in inflation may make investors nervous, which could result in continued volatility and stock market decline. That's a big concern in my opinion, but it's not likely to change my long-term view of the company the way I see it.

My bullish thesis is also built around the Fed cutting rates in 2026. Again, that's not a guarantee. Let's say if there were no cuts, or even discussions about hiking arise, that could severely and negatively impact stock market outlook in the short-term.

While these risks and concerns are important, I still think the market may have already priced in the Robinhood stock price. It underwent significant multiple expansion, although maintained stable business performance. I argue it's due for a bounce, but time will tell.

Outlook Improved: Reasonable Valuation And End To Crypto Winter

I like Robinhood. Quite frankly, I like it a lot. In my opinion it's a well-run business with strong retail-investor base. Based on the recent performance, clients are favoring Robinhood versus available alternatives, and to me that's a significant argument for its long-term thesis. However, when it was a $150 stock, it was difficult to argue for a meaningful stock price increase. Now the valuation seems more reasonable and with tailwinds in-play, I wouldn't be surprised if HOOD outperformed the benchmark from these levels.

I upgraded Robinhood's rating from Sell to Buy. 26 Wall Street analysts imply an average stock price target of $114, indicating 69% upside potential. It resonates with my bullish take, but I personally would expect more conservative stock price appreciation over the next months. I am looking forward to seeing whether my expectations materialize.

data.title: Kuehne+Nagel: Not An Attractive Play For Now
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to revisit Kuehne + Nagel (KHNGY) now that we have the whole year’s performance, to see how well the company did in 2025, and what is still in store in 2026. I am not confident purely in the macroeconomic outlook currently, and for this reason, I am not going to start a position yet.

Since the last time I covered the company, when I gave it a hold rating, its share price has returned flat against SPY’s (SPY) 20% gain.

Briefly on Performance

At the beginning of the year, the company saw some decent growth across all transportation segments. It seems that Q1 ’25 was the strongest quarter of the year, with net turnover increasing 15% compared to the same time last year, while organic growth was 11%. Sea Logistics gained market share and improved yields. Air Logistics saw 5% growth in volumes, as well as gaining market share. Road logistics were already troubled in Q1 due to softer European demand and a lower network utilization rate. Q2 was still positive, but less so. Bet turnover still increased, but it was only 2% y/y. You may ask why the momentum slowed. Well, Q2 is when Liberation Day and subsequent tariff uncertainty kicked in. This uncertainty caused a lot of volatility in demand and rates, especially on US-related lanes. The US dollar weakened quite a bit then, too, and since Sea Logistics is mostly dollar-denominated, FX hurt the financial performance quite a bit.

In the following quarter, Q3, things only got worse for the company, as the slowdown became more obvious. The turnover for nine months came in at only about 3% growth, while for Q3, turnover was down 7%. The yield in Sea Logistics was hit by lower US-bound volumes, while Air Logistics still showed some resilience; however, airfreight was tough overall. In the latest quarter, Q4, we can see that the headline volumes were down another 2%, down 3% q/q for Sea Logistics. Air Logistics began to see volume growth of around 7% y/y, with semiconductors and cloud infrastructure being the main contributors to the revival of the segment. Net turnover for the year came in at around 3% growth.

Surprisingly, I would have expected the company’s profitability to take quite a big hit; however, that was not the case. Don’t get me wrong, overall, margins did suffer. Around 100bps on each segment, except for gross margins, which actually improved. In terms of EBIT, we can see that the company did lose quite a bit of profitability. Company-wide profits declined 14% y/y, with recurring Sea+Air logistics EBIT declining around 19%, bringing EPS down 25% y/y.

Going over the company’s financial position, Kuehne finished the year with CHF750m in cash and equivalents, and no long-term debt, except for some ST interest-bearing liabilities and operating leases. The company has a pretty strong balance sheet. It’s relatively asset-light, which helps its operations. Free cash flow also saw a decent increase y/y to CH917m. The company continues to be an amazing cash-generating machine.

Overall, a lot of the performance was essentially out of the company’s hands. There is very little they can do to stimulate demand if the macroeconomic environment is so unfavorable for carriers and customers alike. I am impressed that the company managed to minimize the impact of the softer demand, particularly well in terms of profitability, and still managed to maintain a strong balance sheet, as well as generate even more cash than last year.

Guidance and the current conflict

First and foremost, let’s dig a little into what the management let us know regarding 2026. Recurring EBIT should be in the range of CHF1.2B-CHF1.4B. The management said that they are being deliberately conservative when giving the range because of the uncertainty of the freight market in 2026. That was a very good idea to be a bit more conservative because now that there are a lot of interruptions for Sea logistics because of the conflict between the US and Iran, which has closed the Strait of Hormuz. This way, the company may not need to adjust its guidance in the future, if it was already conservative, but we won’t know until the drama passes. Just a couple of days ago, the company came out with an update regarding the situation and said that many sea carriers rerouted due to persisting security risks. Nevertheless, there may be some costs mounting due to rising bunker prices, higher war-risk insurance premiums, and additional handling requirements.

The Trump Administration, and mostly just Trump at this point, continues to play with everyone’s patience. One day, he says the deal to end the madness is close, and then he threatens to bring Iran back to the Stone Age. His actions are unpredictable, and apparently, there is going to be at least another 2-3 weeks of this uncertainty. After that, we’ll get some other uncertainty coming up, that’s for sure.

The positives for the year

The company seems to be pretty well prepared to take on the uncertainty. The guidance included a cost savings ramp-up in 2026, which is expected to net an additional CHF100m in savings to begin with. I am sure this is just the beginning, as the only way to combat such uncertainty is to try to cut everywhere it can to maintain its dominance in freight forwarding.

Speaking of market dominance, the management expects to see share gains in Sea and Air logistics even if the overall freight market is flat or modestly up. And now, given the weaker market sentiment, share gains can make a big difference between holding on to the margins the company already has and seeing them erode further.

Contract logistics has been particularly resilient throughout this uncertainty, and I believe it will be the stabilizer in 2026 as well. The segment showed record full-year EBIT for 2025, and the ongoing demand for integrated logistics solutions seems to be leaning towards further growth.

A bit further out, as the management puts it, is the talk of AI and digitalization. I’ve been talking about how digitalization is one of the main ways a company can become more efficient, especially in this freight forwarding market, so I am a little disappointed to that the management doesn’t see any material gains in efficiency from AI and other similar technologies. They expect this to change in 2027, which I hope is actually going to happen. I believe that AI is quite crucial for such a complex forwarding market. Automating repetitive work, improving routing and pricing decisions, as well as making shipments more visible and predictable, are just a few things that I can see making a real impact on the company’s operating efficiency in the long-term. I wish it had focused more on this sooner, but it’s better than never.

Risks and Closing Comments

Besides the positives, there are, of course, some negatives to watch out for. Primarily, which we already touched upon, is the weak freight market. Any further escalation in unrest due to either side will continue to affect the backdrop. The FX headwinds are there, too, because USD-linked activities will depreciate in value as more and more people flee the currency. That will materially affect the company’s EBIT.

For now, I don’t think I would be interested in owning the company purely due to the macroeconomic outlook in 2026. Freight markets have been quite weak overall, with very little growth in the past and little to no growth going forward. The company’s profitability has taken a decent hit, which it cannot afford to keep doing, so I would like to see the profitability story change over the next few quarters.
